name: ZAYTUNA COLLEGE
mission: Zaytuna College aims to educate and prepare morally committed professional, intellectual, and spiritual leaders, who are grounded in the Islamic scholar...
programs: BA in Liberal and Islamic Studies
ZCELL (Center for Ethical Living and Learning)
theoryOfChange: Zaytuna College describes its mission to educate and prepare morally committed professional, intellectual, and spiritual leaders grounded in the Islamic scholarly tradition and conversant with cultural currents and critical ideas shaping modern society; it also describes giving as 'the gift of knowledge' (sadaqah jariya) that will benefit humanity for generations, and states donations help students graduate debt-free.
scoreSummary: ZAYTUNA COLLEGE shows good alignment and moderate impact, with zakat compliance (major risk deductions applied).

evaluationSources: Accreditation (Western Association of Schools and Colleges (WASC))

zakatClaimEvidence: According to many scholars, institutions such as Zaytuna College meet the requirements for zakat eligibility. However, we have chosen to limit the zakat funds only to the total cost of education for zakat eligible students and not for any other institutional expenditures. We take this sacred trust very seriously, so if you choose to give a portion of your zakat to Zaytuna College, we will do our utmost, God willing, to ensure its most judicious and shariah compliant use. Additionally, Zaytuna College has a dedicated 'Zakat Fund' which 'serves as the basis of our financial aid program and is utilized to cover the total cost of educating a zakat-eligible student.' (Source: https://zaytuna.edu/Zakat-Essentials)

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Context dependent profile. Consider cause fit, risk profile, and evidence maturity before deciding.

keyConcerns.headline: Zakat-collecting organization holds 34 months of reserves
keyConcerns.detail: Zakat must be distributed promptly per Islamic jurisprudence. Large reserves raise concerns about timely distribution.

keyConcerns.headline: CEO compensation is 3.0% of revenue ($533,781 on $18,021,649)
keyConcerns.detail: For orgs with revenue $5-50M, CEO pay above 2% of revenue warrants scrutiny.

keyConcerns.headline: Fundraising costs are 28% of total expenses
keyConcerns.detail: High fundraising spend reduces funds available for programs.

components.evidence: Explicit zakat program (+4); Muslim-focused organization (+2); Islamic identity (+1)
components.improvement_suggestion: Clarify zakat allocation policy and eligible use categories for donors.
components.name: Muslim Donor Fit

alignment.rationale: Alignment 29/50: MEDIUM Muslim donor fit, education global cause area

components.improvement_suggestion: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.
components.name: Cost Per Beneficiary

components.improvement_suggestion: Publish a time-bound plan to deploy excess unrestricted reserves into mission delivery, while keeping a clear operating reserve floor.
components.name: Financial Health

components.evidence: Board: 12 members, but For orgs with revenue $5-50M, CEO pay above 2% of revenue warrants scrutiny. — oversight concern
components.name: Governance

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions the program expense ratio as a negative factor, but doesn't explicitly mention the high fundraising costs or CEO compensation as contributing to the moderate score. These are flagged as risks and should be acknowledged in the overall score rationale.

The impact rationale mentions the program expense ratio but doesn't explicitly mention the excessive working capital as a negative factor impacting the score. While the program expense ratio is mentioned, the impact of the excessive working capital on the financial health component (and thus the overall impact score) could be clearer.
Website citation for 'founded_year' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred
Website citation for 'has_audited_financials' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred

We take this sacred trust very seriously, so if you choose to give a portion of your zakat to Zaytuna College, we will do our utmost, God willing, to ensure its most judicious and shariah compliant use. Zaytuna College is zakat-eligible and follows specific asnaf categories for distribution.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The college maintains a moderate overall score reflecting its unique educational niche and strong liquidity, balanced against a program expense ratio that suggests significant resources are directed toward administrative or fundraising infrastructure.
areas_for_improvement: program expense ratio of 56.0% is relatively low compared to specialized educational benchmarks
Limited public data regarding specific long-term cost-per-beneficiary metrics

dimension_explanations.alignment: The college offers a medium donor fit for those seeking to support Islamic higher education and global educational urgency [5]. It is verified as ZAKAT-ELIGIBLE, providing a structured framework for donors to fulfill their religious obligations through student scholarships and institutional support [2][8].
dimension_explanations.impact: Zaytuna College demonstrates direct service impact through the awarding of degrees and its Summer Arabic Intensive program [4][5]. While the college reported a Total Revenue of $18,021,649, its Program Expense Ratio is 56.0%, indicating that just over half of its annual spending is dedicated directly to its core educational programs [1][3].

baseline_narrative.headline: Zaytuna College is the first accredited Muslim liberal arts college in the United States, dedicated to grounding students in both the Islamic scholarly tradition and the Western liberal arts.
strengths: Strong financial liquidity with 33.6 months of working capital available to sustain operations
High fundraising efficiency with a cost of only $0.12 per $1 raised
First accredited Muslim liberal arts college in the United States
baseline_narrative.summary: Located in Berkeley, California, Zaytuna College offers specialized degree programs including a Bachelor of Arts in Liberal Arts and Islamic Studies and a Master of Arts in Islamic Texts [5]. The institution operates as a zakat-eligible entity, maintaining a specific policy to ensure student financial aid aligns with traditional asnaf categories [2][7].

credibility.explanation: Zaytuna holds a <cite id="17">Gold Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite> and maintains <cite id="13">WASC accreditation</cite>, which requires rigorous external audits and governance standards.
credibility.improvement: Credibility could be further enhanced by <cite id="18">publishing board meeting minutes</cite> and more detailed annual impact metrics.

impact.explanation: Impact is driven by direct service delivery, having <cite id="9">awarded 158 degrees</cite> to date. However, the <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 56.0%</cite> is below industry standards.
impact.improvement: The college should <cite id="19">publish a time-bound plan to deploy excess unrestricted reserves</cite> into mission delivery to improve its program ratio.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who want to invest in the long-term intellectual infrastructure of American Islam through an accredited academic institution.
donor_motivations: Value the synthesis of Islamic tradition and Western liberal arts
Want to support the first accredited Muslim college in the US
Prefer giving to institutions with high financial stability
Seek to fulfill zakat through student scholarships
giving_considerations: Gifts contribute to a highly liquid institution with large reserves
Focus is on long-term leadership development rather than immediate relief
Academic accreditation ensures high standards but also higher overhead
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-efficiency direct humanitarian aid or those who prefer their donations to be spent immediately rather than held in reserve.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: The college provides <cite id="13">verified academic outcomes</cite> through its accreditation and tracks <cite id="9">degree completion and digital reach</cite>.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: Zaytuna creates impact by training morally committed leaders through a <cite id="23">dual-tradition curriculum</cite> that prepares them for graduate studies and professional life.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Accreditation ensures that the degrees provided have recognized value in the broader professional and academic world.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While the college has <cite id="8">significant reserves</cite>, its <cite id="15">19.7% growth rate</cite> suggests it is scaling its campus and faculty capacity.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Zaytuna is the <cite id="1">only accredited Muslim liberal arts college</cite> in its peer group, which includes smaller, unaccredited seminaries.

strengths.detail: The college maintains <cite id="8">33.6 months of working capital</cite>, providing a massive buffer against economic volatility compared to most non-profits.
Zaytuna spends only <cite id="12">$0.11 to raise every $1</cite>, significantly outperforming standard efficiency benchmarks.
The institution is <cite id="13">accredited by the WASC Senior College and University Commission</cite>, validating its academic rigor.

strengths_deep_dive: Zaytuna's primary strength lies in its unique market position as a pioneer in Islamic higher education. Since its <cite id="1">founding in 2009</cite>, it has successfully navigated the complex requirements of <cite id="13">regional accreditation</cite> while maintaining a traditional curriculum. Financially, the organization is highly stable, with <cite id="14">net assets growing to $42.5 million</cite> by 2023. This stability is paired with a <cite id="15">3-year revenue CAGR of 19.7%</cite>, indicating strong and consistent donor support for its mission.
rich_narrative.summary: founded in 2009, Zaytuna College stands as the <cite id="1">first accredited Muslim liberal arts college</cite> in the United States, representing a landmark shift in American Islamic higher education. Based in Berkeley, California, the institution seeks to bridge the gap between the Islamic scholarly tradition and Western liberal arts through its <cite id="2">BA in Liberal and Islamic Studies</cite> and <cite id="3">MA in Islamic Texts</cite>. The college reported <cite id="4">total revenue of $18,359,667</cite> in 2023, driven largely by <cite id="5">$15.2 million in contributions</cite>. While the institution maintains a <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 56.0%</cite>, which is lower than the <cite id="7">peer median of 85.3%</cite>, it demonstrates exceptional fiscal stability with <cite id="8">33.6 months of working capital</cite> in reserve. Beyond its physical campus, Zaytuna has achieved significant cultural reach, documenting over <cite id="9">36 million digital views</cite> of its educational content. As a <cite id="10">zakat-eligible institution</cite>, the college maintains a rigorous <cite id="11">zakat allocation policy</cite> that directs funds toward student financial aid across five traditional asnaf categories, including the poor (fuqara) and needy (masakin).
